We've covered all the essential features of Claude CoWork. But now what I want to talk about is probably the most important principle when it comes to using Claude CoWork well and really any AI tool. And the goal of context management is to give Claude the right context at the right time. Modern AI models, they operate on a so-called context window. And all this is is the maximum amount of information the AI can process at a time. In this context window, you'll have things like system instructions. So, these are instructions that OpenAI or Anthropic have written for their specific tools. So, Claude CoWork will have a set of system instructions written by Anthropic. You'll also have global instructions that are written by you. These are going to be instructions that you want to persist across all of your conversations. Also, in the context window will be the connectors available to Claude, all the skills available to Claude, so the names and the descriptions of all these things. And then when you give Claude a request, that needs to go into the context window. As we've seen when doing complex tasks, Claude will do a lot of thinking, it'll call a lot of tools, those tools will have results. And so, all of this is going to live in the context window and it's not uncommon for Claude to call like 10 tools when doing a complex task. So, this is going to be like a really big chunk of what Claude is operating on and only then will Claude generate a actual response to your request. So, all this lives in the context window and the rule here is the better your context, the better your results. And so, there are two key ways that people mismanage the context window. First, maybe they don't give Claude enough context. So, maybe this might be a sparse or vague request or maybe they just didn't set up the right connectors or they didn't make the right files available to Claude. And so, if Claude doesn't have the right connectors, it doesn't have the right skills, it doesn't have enough context in your request, its response isn't going to be that great because it has nothing to work off of. And so, a helpful frame here is to just put yourself into Claude's shoes and just ask yourself, "If someone asked me to do this task with all the context and the tools that I'm giving Claude, would I be able to do it?" And if the answer to that question is no, then that's a sign you need to give Claude more context. The second way people go wrong is like on the opposite extreme. First is people don't give Claude enough context, the second is that they give Claude way too much context. So, I call these mega chats. So, it's like these single long-running conversations about a specific topic. This is understandable because if you're having a conversation, there's a lot of really valuable context that just comes up naturally in that conversation and you don't want Claude to forget it. So, anytime you want to talk more about that topic, whether it's like sales or LinkedIn, learning about AI or whatever else it is, you just go back to that chat and continue the conversation. The problem with this, however, is this idea of so-called context rot, which is this observation that once you start hitting 70-ish percent of the context window, there's a dramatic drop in the quality of Claude's responses. And so, this begs the question, how do we get quality context without the rot? And so, there are three key ways that I'll talk about here. The first are skills. So, we've already seen how skills give Claude the right context at the right time through this idea of progressive disclosure. The second are external files and data sources. So, not all the context needs to live in the context window. You can store things on Notion, you can store things in Obsidian and Confluence or just in Word documents or PowerPoints or text files living on your desktop. Finally, you can use projects. So again, projects are just these folders that either live on your computer or live in the cloud. And then the cool thing about projects is that you can have project-specific instructions, memories, and skills for Claude. Instead of global instructions, you can have project instructions, which are only shown to Claude when it's operating in that specific folder or project. Same thing with memories, so Claude will automatically store things to a text file if it thinks it's relevant. And then you can also have like skills specific to a project. So, maybe I don't want my proposal creator skill to live across all my sessions, I only want it to live in this folder just to kind of limit the amount of noise in the context window when I create fresh sessions. Once you use Claude more and more, you're going to want to start optimizing your workflow. This could be through clever context management, which is the most important thing. But there are also some newer features that can help you in this process of optimizing your workflow with CoWork. The first is scheduled tasks. So, for example, if I don't want to have to keep typing executive briefing every morning and waiting for it to run, I could just set up a scheduled task that every morning at 6:30 a.m., Claude will automatically run the executive briefing skill. So, when I sit down on my laptop, the briefing is already ready to go. Another cool feature is dispatch. Finally, there's this newer feature of live artifacts. And so, you can create custom dashboards and just have them live in Claude.
